metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a minimal TICKET_DIGEST payload schema to the plan (e.g., `{content_hash: str, model: str, digest_hash_version: int, digest: <type>, created_at: HLC}`) so writer and reader share a named contract.
findings.finding: The event payload field names for the freshness tags `model` and `schema`/`DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` are never specified. An executor must invent field names, risking incompatible schemas between the writer (digest_sidecar.py) and the reader (`latest_ticket_digest`) and between this ticket and S1.
findings.location: Scope — `latest_ticket_digest` reader; AC line 'model change, or schema/DIGEST_HASH_VERSION change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either (a) add the drain-skip call site (file + function) to the Scope section and name a test that exercises it without mocking the freshness check, or (b) explicitly mark `test_no_reextract_unchanged` as belonging to S1 and remove it from this ticket's ACs.
findings.finding: AC `test_no_reextract_unchanged` asserts 'the drain skips a ticket whose current content-hash + model/schema tags match its stored digest', but the Scope section names no file or implementation step for drain-skip logic in this ticket. 'Drain' is owned by the S1 dependency; if S1 implements the skip, this AC cannot be verified from steps described here — and if this ticket must implement it, the file target is missing.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — '::test_no_reextract_unchanged'; Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Scope edit target naming the drain/enrichment pipeline file (or function) where the freshness check is performed before invoking S1, so AC #4 has a corresponding implementation anchor.
findings.finding: AC #4 (drain skips unchanged tickets) and its test `test_no_reextract_unchanged` reference drain-integration behavior — checking content-hash + model/schema tags before calling S1 — but the Scope section lists no edit target for this drain logic; no file or function is named where the skip check lives.
findings.location: AC #4 vs Scope (edit targets list)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the fail-closed freshness analogy: `compute_validity` never returns `None` — it always returns `{"valid": False, ...}`; the real pattern is callers pass `{}` as `ticket_state` on read error (see `attest.py` lines 793–796), so specify `digest_sidecar`'s freshness reader returns `{"current": False, ...}` on unreadable state rather than `None`.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'mirroring compute_validity returning None on read error' as the behavioral model for fail-closed freshness, but compute_validity never returns None — it always returns a dict with {valid: bool, reason: str}. The actual fail-closed pattern in the codebase is that callers pass {} as ticket_state on a read error (see attest.py line 794-795), and compute_validity returns {valid: False, ...} from its normal dict paths. The plan's stated analogy is factually wrong about how compute_validity behaves.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Fail-closed freshness' bullet: 'mirroring compute_validity returning None on read error'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'mirroring compute_validity returning None on read error' with: 'mirroring the caller pattern in attest.py: on unreadable state, pass an empty/default hash context to the freshness check so it returns {"current": False} — the freshness function itself always returns a dict, never None.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the Scope edit targets — the `overlap` package directory does not exist and must be created (as a new package) before `digest_sidecar.py` can be imported as `rebar.llm.overlap.digest_sidecar`.
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py` as a concrete edit target, but the `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` package directory does not exist in the codebase. The scope section lists `digest_sidecar.py` but omits creating the parent package (`src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`). Without the `__init__.py`, Python cannot import `digest_sidecar` as `rebar.llm.overlap.digest_sidecar`, so any consumer of the new module will fail at import time.
findings.location: Scope section — 'A latest_ticket_digest(ticket_id) reader … in a new src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet list: '`src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`: new package init (empty or with a short docstring — mirrors the pattern of other `rebar.llm` sub-packages like `plan_review/__init__.py` and `code_review/__init__.py`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the hardcoded `StoreError` message enumeration at `event_append.py` lines 97-103 as a second explicit edit target within that file, updating the prose string to include `TICKET_DIGEST` alongside the frozenset change.
findings.finding: The hardcoded StoreError message string in `event_append.py` (lines 97-103) explicitly enumerates all valid event type names as prose. It is a distinct edit target from the `EVENT_TYPES` frozenset — adding `TICKET_DIGEST` to the frozenset alone leaves the error message stale and missing the new type.
findings.location: src/rebar/_store/event_append.py lines 97-103 (the `raise StoreError(...)` block inside `stage_and_commit`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the `Scope` section, extend the `event_append.py` bullet: 'also update the hardcoded StoreError enumeration string at lines 97-103 to include `"TICKET_DIGEST"`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` as an explicit edit target in the Scope section to complete the new Python package declaration.
findings.finding: The plan places `digest_sidecar.py` in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/` — a directory that does not currently exist. Creating a new Python subpackage requires an `__init__.py` alongside the module file, but the plan names only `digest_sidecar.py` as the edit target without mentioning the package init file.
findings.location: Scope section: 'A `latest_ticket_digest(ticket_id)` reader … in a new `src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the `digest_sidecar.py` bullet: 'also create `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (empty or with a package docstring) to declare the new subpackage'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `TICKET_DIGEST` sidecar event payload schema: enumerate the field names (including the `schema` sentinel string, `content_hash`, `model`, `digest_hash_version`, and the digest payload carrier), their types, and which are required — either inline in the Scope section or as a new `src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest_sidecar.schema.json` edit target.
findings.finding: The TICKET_DIGEST event payload schema (the `data` field stored on disk) is never formally defined: field names, types, required fields, and the schema-sentinel string (analogous to `'plan_review_result_v1'` in the REVIEW_RESULT precedent) are all absent. The `latest_ticket_digest` reader needs a sentinel string to guard against foreign-schema payloads (the walk-back logic in the REVIEW_RESULT precedent keys on `payload.get('schema') == 'plan_review_result_v1'`); without this definition, two implementors will independently invent incompatible strings.
findings.location: Scope section — `digest_sidecar.py` description; What section — 'A scan reduces to the most-recent TICKET_DIGEST per ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a payload table to the Scope section, e.g.: `{schema: 'ticket_digest_v1', ticket_id: str, content_hash: str, model: str, digest_hash_version: int, digest: <ticket_digest schema object>}`, and name the sentinel string `'ticket_digest_v1'` so the reader's schema guard is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the freshness decision rule with the payload schema: name the exact stored field keys for `model` and `DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` (e.g. `'model'` and `'digest_hash_version'`) alongside the `content_hash` field in the payload definition so all three staleness triggers are unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: The freshness decision rule references `model` and `schema`/`DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` as three independent staleness triggers, but the field names that carry these values in the stored payload are never co-located with the freshness protocol. An executor implementing `latest_ticket_digest` must invent field names for `model` and `digest_hash_version` without a normative specification.
findings.location: What section — 'the digest is CURRENT iff its stored `content_hash` (and `model`/`schema` version tags) match'; AC line — 'Freshness flips stale on ANY of: content-hash mismatch, `model` change, or `schema`/`DIGEST_HASH_VERSION` change'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the triggering mechanism for prune-on-archive/delete: name the call-site (e.g., the archive command, the drain loop, or a post-archive hook) that invokes `prune(keep=0)` or equivalent for TICKET_DIGEST when a ticket is archived or deleted.
findings.finding: The prune-on-archived/deleted-ticket behavior names the desired outcome ('a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by `prune(keep=1)` retention') but never specifies the triggering mechanism: which code path calls `prune` after an archive or delete event, and at what point in the lifecycle. Without this, the prune logic has no defined invocation contract.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prune/retire: a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by a `prune(keep=1)` retention'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify who triggers `prune(keep=1)` for archived/deleted tickets and when (e.g., 'the drain calls prune after emit on every write; archived-ticket cleanup is handled by the same drain loop that already skips archived tickets — it calls prune if a digest exists but the ticket is no longer active').
findings.finding: The plan states 'a digest for a deleted/archived ticket is dropped by a prune(keep=1)' but does not specify WHEN or BY WHOM `prune` is called for a deleted/archived ticket — it is unclear whether this is triggered by the drain, a separate cleanup job, on-read, or by the archiving operation itself.
findings.location: Scope / 'Prune/retire' paragraph; `src/rebar/llm/overlap/digest_sidecar.py`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit post-rollback state assertion: enumerate that after rolling back to a pre-`TICKET_DIGEST` binary, on-disk `TICKET_DIGEST` files trigger `fsck` 'newer-than-me' warnings (because `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` is reverted), and specify whether the back-out path requires pruning those files before rollback or whether the warnings are accepted as a bounded rollout-window condition.
findings.finding: The plan asserts AC3 that 'an older binary preserves-and-ignores [TICKET_DIGEST] with no fsck WARN,' but never explicitly enumerates the post-rollback schema state. On rollback to a pre-TICKET_DIGEST binary, TICKET_DIGEST is absent from that binary's _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, so is_unknown_newer_type() returns True for every TICKET_DIGEST file on disk, firing fsck 'newer-than-me' warnings — the exact behavior AC3 claims will not occur. The plan's 'upgrade reconcile hosts first' note acknowledges rollout order but does not define the concrete disk+binary state after a rollback, nor does it confirm TICKET_DIGEST events on disk are harmless to that older binary's other write paths.
findings.location: AC3 ('an older binary preserves-and-ignores it with no fsck WARN') and Scope section ('add TICKET_DIGEST to _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback / back-out' subsection stating: 'Rolling back this change reverts _NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES, so existing TICKET_DIGEST files on disk will trigger fsck newer-than-me warnings on the older binary. This is bounded and safe (the files are sidecar-only and do not affect reducer state), but operators should be aware. If a clean rollback is required, run `rebar digest prune --all` before reverting the binary, which removes all TICKET_DIGEST files from the shared store.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a named test node (e.g., `::test_older_binary_preserves_and_ignores`) to the Testing section and map it to the third AC bullet so the fsck/preserve behavior has a concrete proving command.
findings.finding: The AC 'older binary preserves-and-ignores the event (fsck no WARN)' is stated in both the Testing bullets and the ACs but no named `::test_*` node is provided, leaving the proving command absent.
findings.location: Testing section — 'older binary preserves-and-ignores the event (fsck no WARN)'; Acceptance Criteria — third bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/unit/test_ticket_digest_store.py::test_older_binary_preserves_and_ignores` that loads a store containing a TICKET_DIGEST event with a reducer instance that does NOT list it in KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES, runs fsck, and asserts no WARN is emitted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the specific file(s) (e.g. `src/rebar/_reads.py`, `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py`, or a CLI display layer) where the `show` digest-presence + freshness rendering will be implemented.
findings.finding: The plan states '`show` renders digest presence + freshness only' but names no specific file or function to implement this rendering. The `show` path runs through `_reads.py` → `_engine_support.reads.show_state` → reducer, and the digest is a sidecar (not in compiled state). Rendering freshness at `show` time requires a code change outside the named scope, but no edit target is identified.
findings.location: Scope section: '`show` renders digest presence + freshness only (not the raw digest text); the raw digest is available to retrieval.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` normalization in (or explicitly diff from) the existing `_ticket_text` function in `_engine_support/gates.py`, specifying exact behavior for absent fields (None vs ''), empty comment bodies, and trailing whitespace so all callers hash identically.
findings.finding: The plan defines its own normalization for DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS ('strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n"') without referencing the existing canonical implementation in _engine_support/gates.py::_ticket_text, which performs the identical operation (title + description + comment bodies, "\n".join). If the implementation diverges on edge cases — empty vs. absent description, comments with empty bodies, or Unicode whitespace — the content_hash will differ from what S1's enrich op computed, silently marking all cached digests stale or (worse) accepting a stale digest as fresh.
findings.location: Scope section — digest_sidecar.py: 'content_hash helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS = title + description + comment bodies, normalized: strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n")'
findings.suggested_fix: In digest_sidecar.py, import and call `_ticket_text(state)` (or extract it to a shared util) as the canonical input to the content hash, rather than re-implementing field extraction. If the plan intentionally differs (e.g., excludes comment bodies for stability), document the divergence explicitly and add a cross-reference so future changes to _ticket_text do not silently break cache hit rates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the prune semantics for archived/deleted tickets: explicitly specify whether `prune` uses `keep=0` for archived tickets (full removal) or `keep=1` everywhere (and correct the 'dropped/pruned' language in Scope, Testing, and AC #7 accordingly).
findings.finding: prune(keep=1) retains one record per ticket, but the Scope, Testing, and AC #7 all assert that an archived/deleted ticket's digest is 'dropped'/'pruned' — these are contradictory: keep=1 means one digest survives, not zero.
findings.location: Scope (Prune/retire paragraph) vs Testing bullet 'archived ticket's digest pruned' vs AC #7
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope prune/retire paragraph, replace 'dropped by a prune(keep=1) retention' with 'dropped by a prune(keep=0) call when the ticket is archived/deleted, vs prune(keep=1) for active tickets to retain only the freshest digest.' Update AC #7 and the Testing bullet to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical comment ordering rule in the DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS definition (e.g., 'comments sorted by HLC timestamp ascending, ties broken by event log position') so all clients produce identical hashes.
findings.finding: The DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS normalization rule is incomplete: comment ordering (insertion order vs. HLC timestamp order) is unspecified. Two clients enriching the same ticket at slightly different times could iterate comments in different orders, producing different content-hashes and perpetually invalidating each other's cached digests.
findings.location: Scope — `content_hash` helper over DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS definition; digest_sidecar.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the normalization spec: 'Comment bodies are included in HLC-ascending order (event-log position as tiebreaker), so that any two clients reading the same store snapshot produce identical hashes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify or replace the bespoke `content_hash` normalization (strip trailing whitespace + newline-join) in `digest_sidecar.py`: either adopt `rebar._store.canonical.content_hash` (the established codebase primitive already used for stable content hashing in `workflow/schema.py` and the reconciler), or document explicitly why a different normalization is required and pin its exact specification so all clients converge.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a novel custom content-hash normalization (strip trailing whitespace, join with '\n') for DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS, without acknowledging or justifying its divergence from the codebase's established `canonical.content_hash` primitive (`src/rebar/_store/canonical.py:74`) that is already used for stable content hashing in `workflow/schema.py`, the reconciler, and the conflict resolver.
findings.location: ## What / Scope — `digest_sidecar.py` `content_hash` helper description: "strip trailing whitespace, join with '\n'"
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the custom normalization with `from rebar._store.canonical import content_hash as _canonical_hash` and compute `_canonical_hash({"title": title, "description": description, "comments": comment_bodies})` — this is deterministic, sort-keyed, and already trusted by the codebase for exactly this purpose. Update `DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` to be a documented tuple of field names and let `canonical.content_hash` handle serialization.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a canonical comment-ordering rule to the DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS normalization spec (e.g., 'comment bodies sorted by HLC timestamp ascending, event-log position as tiebreaker') so all clients produce identical hashes for the same ticket state.
findings.finding: The DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS normalization rule omits a comment-ordering specification. The plan states 'title + description + comment bodies, normalized: strip trailing whitespace, join with `\n`' but gives no canonical ordering for comment bodies. Two clients enriching the same ticket at slightly different times may iterate comments in different orders (insertion order vs. HLC order), producing different `content_hash` values and treating each other's cached digest as perpetually stale.
findings.location: Scope section — '`content_hash` helper over a NAMED, versioned field set (`DIGEST_HASH_FIELDS` = title + description + comment bodies, normalized: strip trailing whitespace, join with "\n")'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the normalization spec: 'Comment bodies are included in ascending HLC-timestamp order (event-file name order as tiebreaker), ensuring any two clients reading the same store snapshot produce byte-identical hashes.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the drain handles `StoreError` on TICKET_DIGEST write (e.g., log-and-continue so enrichment result is not lost, with a counter/log entry so operators can observe the failure rate).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `stage_and_commit` raises `StoreError` on failure but does not define how the enrichment drain responds — whether it retries, logs-and-continues, or propagates the exception. A swallowed or unhandled StoreError would silently skip persisting the digest with no operator signal.
findings.location: Scope / `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` description; Testing section (no test for write-failure path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'On StoreError the drain logs the error at WARNING level with ticket_id and skips caching (the digest is not lost — it was already used for retrieval — but re-enrichment will occur next run). No retry; the next drain cycle is the retry boundary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC with a proving command for the `show` rendering (e.g., `rebar show <ticket_id>` output contains a freshness indicator line when a digest is present and marks it stale after a field mutation).
findings.finding: The `show` command rendering (digest presence + freshness indicator) is described in scope but has no acceptance criterion and no proving command, leaving a user-facing behavior unverified.
findings.location: Scope — '`show` renders digest presence + freshness only'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (and corresponding unit/integration test) covering the `show` command rendering digest presence and freshness status, so this Scope deliverable has a verifiable AC.
findings.finding: The Scope describes `show` rendering digest presence + freshness as a deliverable ('show renders digest presence + freshness only (not the raw digest text)'), but no acceptance criterion covers this behavior.
findings.location: Scope ('show renders…' sentence) vs Acceptance Criteria (AC #1–#7)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `show <ticket>` displays digest presence and freshness (CURRENT/STALE) without exposing raw digest text; verified by `::test_show_digest_freshness_indicator`.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a corresponding unit/integration test) that verifies `show` renders digest presence and freshness status for a ticket with a current digest and for one with a stale digest.
findings.finding: The `show` rendering of digest presence + freshness is a non-deferred goal stated in the body ('show renders digest presence + freshness only') but has no corresponding AC or test — making it unverifiable at delivery.
findings.location: ## What — 'show renders digest presence + freshness only'; Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `show <ticket-id>` displays digest presence and freshness (CURRENT/STALE) without raw digest text; verified by `::test_show_digest_freshness_display`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC + named test (e.g., `test_show_digest_presence`) covering `show` rendering of: (a) current digest, (b) stale digest, and (c) no digest present — ensuring the user-visible freshness indicator is behaviorally verified.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new user-facing `show` output (digest presence + freshness indicator) but names zero tests for it — no happy-path, no stale-vs-fresh rendering, no missing-digest case.
findings.location: What section: 'show renders digest presence + freshness only (not the raw digest text)'; Testing section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers show output)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Testing section: `test_show_digest_current` — ticket with a fresh digest shows presence indicator; `test_show_digest_stale` — ticket with a stale digest shows stale label; `test_show_digest_absent` — ticket with no TICKET_DIGEST event omits the block silently. Add a corresponding AC: 'show output is verified by ::test_show_digest_presence/stale/absent'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a lightweight ADR or `CLAUDE.md` entry documenting the LLM-sidecar pattern (use `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES`, freshness-on-read, `prune(keep=1)`) so future sidecar authors have a canonical reference.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel reusable architectural pattern (LLM-sidecar + content-hash freshness + `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` registration) that crosses multiple components, but does not call for any ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture the decision rationale for future contributors.
findings.location: ## Scope / ## Why — no documentation artifact specified
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph entry to `CLAUDE.md` (or `docs/adr/NNN-llm-sidecar-pattern.md`) covering: (1) why `_NON_REPLAY_KNOWN_TYPES` not `KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES`, (2) freshness-on-read rationale, (3) `emit`+`prune(keep=1)` retention contract, and reference `REVIEW_RESULT` + `TICKET_DIGEST` as canonical examples.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: at minimum, log at DEBUG on cache-hit (skip), WARNING on write failure, and INFO on prune; optionally emit counters for digest_written / digest_cache_hit / digest_stale.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation is specified for any new failure point introduced by this plan: digest write failures, stale-digest detections, enrichment skips (cache hits), or prune operations. Operators have no way to monitor cache effectiveness or detect systematic failures.
findings.location: Scope section; Testing section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the title (or add a clarifying note in ## What) to reflect that the record is keyed by ticket_id with a content-hash FIELD used for freshness — e.g., 'Persist ticket digest as a content-hash-tagged sidecar record on the ticket (shared cache)'.
findings.finding: The title says 'content-hash-keyed record' implying the content-hash IS the lookup key, but the body specifies lookup by ticket_id with latest-by-HLC resolution — the hash is a freshness field, not the key. This misrepresents the data model to anyone reading only the title.
findings.location: Title: 'Persist ticket digest as a content-hash-keyed record on the ticket (shared cache)' vs. ## What: 'a scan reduces to the most-recent TICKET_DIGEST per ticket (by HLC order)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename title to: 'Persist ticket digest as a content-hash-tagged sidecar record on the ticket (shared cache)' and/or add a one-line note in ## What: 'The record is keyed by ticket_id (latest-by-HLC); `content_hash` is a payload field used for freshness comparison, not the storage key.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the 'latest-by-HLC wins on read' language to specify that the reader uses filename-timestamp sort (matching `sidecar.py`'s `sorted(files)` + `reversed()` pattern), and note that for current 19-digit HLC prefixes this equals integer sort — or specify use of `reducer._sort.prefix_ts` for future-proof integer ordering.
findings.finding: The plan states 'latest-by-HLC wins on read' for concurrent same-ticket digest appends, but the implementation it is modeled on (sidecar.py::latest_review_result) uses Python's lexicographic sorted() on filenames — not integer-prefix HLC sort. For current 19-digit HLC timestamps string sort == integer sort, but this is an incidental property of today's timestamp width, not a specified contract. If a future timestamp format changes width (or the sidecar reader is written using integer sort for correctness), the plan's 'HLC wins' language will mislead implementers into assuming a stronger ordering guarantee than the actual reader provides.
findings.location: What section: 'Two concurrent same-ticket digests from two clients are safe — both are TICKET_DIGEST appends, latest-by-HLC wins on read, idempotent.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
